To find the time \( t \) when the output voltage \( v_{\text{OUT}} \) crosses -10 V, we need to solve for \( t \) in the equation \( v_{\text{OUT}} = -10 \) V using the given expression \( v_{\text{OUT}} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \left( v_i + \frac{V_{\text{IN}}}{C_1 R_1} t \right) \).

Given:
\( v_{\text{OUT}} = -10 \) V
\( v_i = -2 \) V
\( R_1 = 10 \) kΩ
\( R_2 = 100 \) kΩ
\( C_1 = 1 \) µF

First, substitute the known values into the equation:
\[ -10 = -\frac{100 \times 10^3}{10 \times 10^3} \left( -2 + \frac{12}{1 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^3} t \right) \]

Simplify the equation:
\[ -10 = -10 \left( -2 + 12000 t \right) \]

Distribute the -10:
\[ -10 = 20 - 120000 t \]

Rearrange the equation to solve for \( t \):
\[ 120000 t = 20 + 10 \]
\[ 120000 t = 30 \]

Divide both sides by 120000:
\[ t = \frac{30}{120000} \]

Convert the time from seconds to milliseconds:
\[ t = \frac{30}{120000} \times 1000 \]

Calculate the result:
\[ t = 0.25 \]

Therefore, the output voltage \( v_{\text{OUT}} \) crosses -10 V at \( t = 0.25 \) ms.
